_comment: Editable config for Layer 3 (Confirmed Cases). Source = IDSP Weekly Outbreak Reports (Ministry of Health & Family Welfare). Two views: (1) the SEASON retrospective -- build_layer3_season.py pulls every weekly PDF across the monsoon window of `season.year`, parses each, and writes the week-on-week series to data/confirmed_season.json (the dashboard's main Layer 3 view); (2) the LATEST-report tracker -- build_layer3.py grabs just the newest published report to data/confirmed_cases.json (a small 'this year so far' strip, with the source's own lag shown). IDSP data lags badly (often weeks), so 'this week' framing is dishonest; the season retrospective is the real story and the backtest substrate. Change diseases/states/thresholds freely; no code edits needed.
_manual_pdf_url_comment: Optional hard override. If set (non-empty), the live source SKIPS listing-page discovery and downloads exactly this PDF (IDSP server or Google Drive share link both work). Use it to pin a specific week or to work around a discovery break. Leave empty for normal newest-week auto-discovery.
_fixture_comment: Saved real weekly PDF for the offline `fixture` source (regression-tests the real parser without network). Path is relative to the project root.
_diseases_comment: Target conditions to surface on the dashboard. `match` = case-insensitive substrings tested against the PDF's cleaned Disease/Illness text (any one hits). `match_all` = groups where EVERY token must be present (order-free) -- used for 'Fever with Rash' because government PDFs render it as 'Fever with Rash', 'Fever and Rash', 'Fever & Rash', or, when the wrap drops a word, plain 'Fever Rash'. The first target that matches wins; anything matching none is kept as disease_key='other' so the all-disease total stays honest, but only these four are highlighted. `headline` is shown first / used as the default sort. (Acute Febrile Illness was dropped: it reported zero outbreaks across the 2025 season, so it only added an empty column/card.)
_states_comment: India states + union territories. Used to normalize the messy state text the PDF parser pulls out (government PDFs wrap names mid-word, e.g. 'Chhattisg arh'), by matching on a space/punctuation-insensitive key. Same list as Layer 1/2. `code` is the ISO 3166-2:IN sub-region code; `idsp` is the 2-letter prefix IDSP uses inside its outbreak IDs (State/District/Year/Week/number) where known -- used only as a low-confidence fallback when the state text cannot be matched (IDSP's own prefixes are occasionally inconsistent, so parsed text always wins).

states.name: Dadra and Nagar Haveli and Daman and Diu

sanity._comment: MANDATORY data-quality guard (the single most important Layer 3 requirement). Because we parse government PDFs whose format drifts week to week, the build ABORTS and writes nothing if the parse looks broken: fewer than min_outbreaks total outbreak rows recovered, no Unique-ID anchors found at all (min_id_anchors), or no reporting week identified (require_report_week). A format change must NEVER silently feed a branded dashboard. min_target_outbreaks can be 0: a real quiet week may legitimately have no dengue/malaria/chikungunya/fever-rash outbreak, and that is not a parser failure.

season._comment: The monsoon-season retrospective window for build_layer3_season.py. It enumerates `year`'s weekly reports, keeps the weeks whose ISO span overlaps [window_start, window_end], downloads + parses each (both IDSP-server and Google-Drive hosts), and aggregates a week-on-week series. PDFs are cached under cache_dir (gitignored) so re-runs are fast and polite to the server. The season build ABORTS if fewer than min_weeks_parsed weeks come back clean. start_month/end_month mirror Layer 2's season block so the two layers line up for the backtest.
